titleStmt.title: Letter from Sir Wilfrid Spender to his wife Lady Lillian Spender, 10 August 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Wilfrid Bliss Spender

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from sir Wilfrid Bliss Spender (1876-1960) to his wife, Lady Lillian Spender, née Dean, (1880-1968). In this letter, Spender writes about recently received correspondence and mutual acquaintances. He also discusses the nature of his position in the office, Lillian's social and working life, and financial matters.This letter is part of a series of correspondence from Sir Wilfrid Bliss Spender to his wife Lady Lillian Spender. The letters date mainly from 1915 to 1918, when Sir Wilfrid was serving in France. The letters concern politics, the Irish question, personal life, and Wilfrid's experience of the Great War. Sir Wilfrid Spender (1876 - 1960) was a member of the British Army until 1913 when controversy surrounding his signature of the Ulster Solemn League and Covenant resulted in his resignation. Spender was called up for military service in 1914 and from 1915 served in the 36th (Ulster) Division. Throughout the war, Spender remained in contact with the Ulster Unionist leadership, and consistently opposed Home Rule and the six-county solution. Lady Lillian served in the Ulster Volunteer Nursing Corps during the war, and was an organiser of the Ulster Division Comforts Fund.

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
What anniversaries are coming? 10 Aug My Pr I was rewarded for not grumbling by getting 2 fat
letters today and some from the family. I do hope you
did not give them any hint that I was expecting their
congratulations â as they 3 have all now written! This was
very far from what I meant when I wrote to you, which
was merely to find out how they took it with you. I
thought perhaps they would not like you becoming a temporary
dowager! There was no expectation of any letter from any
of them but they are very welcome all the same It is not easy to explain my position. Aspinall whom
I succeeded has gone to a Division. He was previously a
Brigadier in the Dardanelles. I should say that at GHQ
a GSO1 of a Div is regarded as the better and that outside
the GSO1 at HQ is looked upon as the best. My own job is
a peculiar one and I think only of a temporary character. With the dispatch done if I can complete it! it will be
easier but at present that is rather a weight in
addition to my other work. I had a letter from Reg
today dated the 5th. I am very sorry Cole Hamilton
the Major has gone as he was particularly nice &
would have kept an eye on Rex. His CO needs a
rest â a good chap but now full of little cares &
worries trying to do everything himself & not succeeding. The PR does not lack invitations when the bw is away
as for my pawmarks in the communiquÃ©s sometimes they
are quite clearly imprinted, but often they are much rubbed out
or cut down. Our bank balance won't be so big when you hear that
Â£160 more has gone to Carnsmerryand probably 300 to Magnus
for war insurance later, but there is quite enough for a Pr
to play with when she can get away.'Cum grano Seely's'' It is rather serious being turned out by Mrs KS but
I wonder she and the other members of your Committee have
remained interested so long. Lady Lonsdale does not seem to
have rubbed you up lately. I think I am more likely to growl
than laugh audibly thank you. I must return to work. Must pop off or I shall get no time out at all. Your bw WBSpender
